title: 10 best herbal teas for periodontal disease
intro: While more research is needed, some herbal teas may have properties that could potentially benefit individuals with periodontal disease. For instance, green tea is rich in antioxidants called catechins, which may help reduce inflammation and combat harmful bacteria in the mouth. Chamomile tea, known for its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, could also potentially soothe gum irritation and help maintain oral health. Additionally, echinacea tea has been suggested to boost the immune system, which might aid in fighting off periodontal infections. However, it's crucial to remember that herbal teas should not replace regular dental care and professional treatment. Always consult with a dental healthcare provider for personalized advice.

teas.tea_desc: Chamomile tea contains powerful anti-inflammatory properties due to the presence of flavonoids, which reduce inflammation in the gums caused by periodontal disease. Its antimicrobial properties, derived from the compound Chamazulene, inhibit the growth of harmful bacteria in the mouth that leads to periodontal disease. Chamomile tea also promotes mouth healing through its ability to stimulate the growth of new cells, which helps in the repair of damaged gum tissues. Furthermore, the soothing effect of chamomile helps alleviate mouth pain and soreness, providing relief to those suffering from periodontal disease. Lastly, regular consumption of chamomile tea strengthens the immune system, enhancing the body's ability to fight off oral infections that cause periodontal disease.
tea_parts: Flowers: Chamomile flowers contain anti-inflammatory properties that help in reducing pain and swelling in the gums.
Leaves: Rich in antioxidants, chamomile leaves help in preventing bacterial infection, a common cause of periodontal disease.
Rhyzomes: Chamomile rhyzomes have antimicrobial properties that aid in controlling plaque, a primary cause of periodontal disease.
Stems: The antiseptic properties found in chamomile stems assist in fighting oral infections.
Roots: Chamomile roots contain azulene, which provides anti-inflammatory and antiphlogistic effects, helpful for treating gum inflammation.
tea_recipe: Boil 8 ounces of water in a kettle or pot, allowing it to reach a rolling boil for full mineral exposure.
Add 1-2 teaspoons of dried chamomile flowers to a tea infuser or teapot, ensuring proper dosage for effective anti-inflammatory properties.
Pour the boiling water over the chamomile flowers, allowing them to steep for 5-10 minutes to extract the beneficial compounds.
Remove the tea infuser or strain the liquid to discard the spent flowers, preventing any potential debris from entering the final drink.
Enjoy the warm chamomile tea up to three times daily, promoting periodontal health through its soothing and calming effects.
tea_constituents: Chamazulene: This medicinal constituent has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce redness and swelling in the gums associated with periodontal disease.
Apigenin: Apigenin has antibacterial properties that can help control the growth of harmful bacteria in the mouth, which can contribute to periodontal disease.
Luteolin: Luteolin has been shown to inhibit the production of prostaglandins, which are involved in the inflammatory response associated with periodontal disease.
Bisabolol: Bisabolol has been shown to promote wound healing, which can be beneficial for treating periodontal disease and promoting gum tissue regeneration.
Matricin: Matricin is a precursor to chamazulene, and it also has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce the symptoms of periodontal disease.

teas.tea_desc: Echinacea tea contains active compounds, such as alkamides and polysaccharides, which demonstrate potent immunostimulatory properties. These compounds activate the immune system, increasing the production of white blood cells and enhancing their ability to fight infections. As a result, echinacea tea helps reduce the severity of periodontal disease by diminishing bacterial load and inflammation in the gum tissue. Moreover, echinacea's anti-inflammatory properties help control the production of prostaglandins and cytokines, which are pro-inflammatory molecules contributing to periodontal tissue destruction. Regular consumption of echinacea tea bolsters the body's defense mechanisms, promoting overall oral health and accelerating the healing of inflamed gums and periodontal tissues.
tea_parts: Roots: Containing active compounds that fight off oral bacteria, roots are effective in decreasing inflammation and preventing infections in the gums.
Flowers: Rich in antioxidants, flowers help to reduce oxidative stress and promote healing in the gums and surrounding tissues.
Leaves: Packed with tannins that have astringent properties, leaves help to tighten gum tissue, reduce gum bleeding, and soothe irritation.
Buds: High in essential oils that have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, buds aid in preventing plaque buildup, freshening breath, and promoting oral health.
tea_recipe: In a saucepan, combine 2 cups of water and 2 tablespoons of dried echinacea flowers and leaves.
Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 15 minutes.
Remove the saucepan from heat and let the tea cool down for 5 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the solid parts.
Drink the tea while it's still warm, up to three times a day for periodontal disease treatment.
tea_constituents: Cichoric Acid: This polyphenol has been shown to have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce the redness and swelling associated with periodontal disease.
Epicatechin Gallate: A type of flavonoid, epicatechin gallate has been found to exhibit antibacterial effects, potentially aiding in the prevention of periodontal disease caused by harmful bacteria.
Rutin: A flavonoid glycoside, rutin has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help protect the gums from damage caused by periodontal disease.
Chicoric Acid: Another polyphenol, chicoric acid has been found to have potential immunomodulatory effects, which can help regulate the body's immune response to periodontal disease.
Kaempferol: A flavonol, kaempferol has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help reduce the symptoms of periodontal disease.

teas.tea_desc: Goldenseal tea contains berberine, a powerful alkaloid, which demonstrably exhibits antimicrobial properties. This potent compound targets periodontal disease-causing bacteria, effectively reducing their colonization. Goldenseal tea's anti-inflammatory qualities, derived from its berberine content, alleviate periodontal tissue swelling and inflammation. Moreover, goldenseal tea's astringent properties, owing to the tannins present, help control gum bleeding. Consequently, goldenseal tea bolsters overall oral health by promoting periodontal tissue healing and deterring the progression of periodontal disease.
tea_parts: Roots: Goldenseal roots contain berberine, a compound with potent anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, making them effective in managing periodontal disease.
Rhizomes: The rhizomes of goldenseal, a horizontal stem that grows underground, have rich alkaloid content, including berberine, which helps reduce bacteria responsible for gum inflammation.
Leaves: Goldenseal leaves possess antimicrobial and astringent qualities that can help promote gum tissue health and alleviate periodontal disease symptoms.
Flowers: Although rarely used, goldenseal flowers contain beneficial compounds that can support oral health, contributing to the reduction of periodontal disease-causing bacteria.
tea_recipe: Measure - Use a teaspoon to measure out two Goldenseal root fragments (500-600mg each) and place them in a tea infuser or teapot.
Heat - Boil eight ounces of water and pour it over the Goldenseal root in the infuser or teapot.
Steep - Steep the Goldenseal root in the hot water for 10-15 minutes to extract the beneficial compounds.
Strain - Carefully remove the infuser and discard the used Goldenseal root, or pour the tea through a strainer into a cup.
Serve - Enjoy the warm Goldenseal tea up to three times a day, swishing it around your mouth before swallowing.
tea_constituents: Berberine: Has anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties that help reduce gum inflammation and fight harmful bacteria in the mouth, contributing to periodontal disease.
Hydrastine: Aids in reducing inflammation and helps to constrict blood vessels, which may help control gum bleeding associated with periodontal disease.
Canadine: Contributes to goldenseal's antimicrobial effects, helping to inhibit the growth of periodontal disease-causing bacteria.
Coptisine: Exhibits anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, supporting gum health and fighting periodontal disease-related infections.

teas.tea_desc: Myrrh tea's antiseptic properties, proven by numerous scientific studies, destroy harmful bacteria in the mouth. This plant's resin contains active compounds like terpenoids and sesquiterpenes that directly combat periodontal disease-causing microorganisms. Furthermore, myrrh tea's anti-inflammatory abilities reduce gum inflammation, a common symptom of periodontal disease. Additionally, myrrh tea promotes mouth tissue regeneration, healing damaged gums affected by periodontal disease.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of the myrrh plant contain compounds with potent anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, making them effective for treating periodontal disease.
Rhyzomes: These horizontal underground stems have similar medicinal properties as roots and are used to make tea that helps reduce gum inflammation and fight infections causing periodontal disease.
Bark: The bark of the myrrh tree is rich in antioxidants that help in healing gum tissues and preventing further damage in periodontal disease.
Buds: Myrrh buds are potent sources of antiseptic and analgesic properties, which are beneficial in managing pain and preventing infections in periodontal disease.

Seeds: Myrrh seeds contain essential oils and resins that are used to make teas with potent antimicrobial effects, aiding in treating periodontal disease by inhibiting the growth of harmful bacteria in the mouth.
tea_recipe: In a pot, boil 2 cups of water for 5 minutes.
Add 1 teaspoon of dried myrrh resin to the boiling water.
Reduce heat and let it simmer for 15 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the resin, then let it cool.
Drink 1/2 cup of the myrrh tea, three times a day, after meals.
tea_constituents: Furanodienes: Furanodienes, such as curzerene and miroestrol, have anti-inflammatory properties which may help reduce periodontal tissue inflammation, thereby alleviating periodontal disease symptoms.
Sesquiterpenes: Sesquiterpenes, including elemene and α-humulene, exhibit antimicrobial effects, potentially targeting periodontal pathogens that contribute to periodontal disease progression.

Flavonoids: Flavonoids, such as isorhamnetin and rutin, possess antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, aiding in managing oxidative stress and inflammation in periodontal tissues affected by periodontal disease.

Lignans: Lignans, like sesamin and syringaresinol, demonstrate anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities, potentially improving periodontal health by tackling the underlying causes of periodontal disease.

Phenolic acids: Phenolic acids, such as caffeic acid and ferulic acid, contribute to the antioxidant properties of myrrh, protecting periodontal tissues from oxidative damage caused by periodontal disease.

teas.tea_desc: Turmeric tea contains curcumin, a compound with proven anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. These properties help reduce inflammation in the gums, a key symptom of periodontal disease. Curcumin also inhibits the growth of harmful bacteria that cause gum infections, promoting healthier oral flora. Furthermore, it prevents the breakdown of collagen, a protein that supports gum tissue, thereby strengthening periodontal tissues. Lastly, curcumin's antioxidant effects protect gum tissues from further damage, contributing to overall oral health.
tea_parts: Rhizomes: The underground stem of turmeric contains curcumin, a compound with strong anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help reduce inflammation and improve gum health in periodontal disease.
Roots: The turmeric roots, closely related to rhizomes, also contain curcumin, making it effective in reducing periodontal inflammation and improving overall oral health.
Leaves: Turmeric leaves, rich in antioxidants, are used to make medicinal tea that helps soothe gum irritation, minimize inflammation, and promote healing in periodontal disease.

Flowers: Infrequently used but valuable, turmeric flowers contain curcumin and other bioactive compounds, making them useful for making medicinal tea to alleviate symptoms of periodontal disease and support gum health.
tea_recipe: In a saucepan, combine 4 cups of water and 1 teaspoon of ground turmeric, bringing it to a boil.
Reduce heat and let it simmer for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove turmeric particles, then add honey and lemon juice to taste.
Warm up the tea if necessary, then drink it while still warm.
Repeat this process 1-2 times a day, preferably after meals.
tea_constituents: Curcumin: This compound has anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties that help reduce gum inflammation and fight periodontal pathogens.
Turmerone: With strong anti-inflammatory effects, turmerone helps decrease inflammation in the gums, promoting better periodontal health.
Bisdemethoxycurcumin: This compound shows potential for reducing periodontal inflammation and has been found to inhibit the activation of certain molecules that contribute to periodontal disease.
Demethoxycurcumin: This curcuminoid exhibits anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, making it beneficial in managing periodontal disease.
Essential oils: The volatile oils found in turmeric have antimicrobial properties that aid in controlling the growth of bacteria responsible for periodontal disease.

teas.tea_desc: Ginger tea's antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties, established through scientific research, inhibit the growth of harmful bacteria in the mouth. This herbal remedy reduces inflammation and pocket depth in gums, both of which are key indicators of periodontal disease. Furthermore, ginger tea's active compounds, such as gingerols and shogaols, neutralize oral pathogens, promoting healthier gums and overall oral hygiene. By sipping ginger tea, individuals experience a decrease in the severity of periodontal disease symptoms due to ginger's natural healing abilities. Lastly, a consistent intake of ginger tea bolsters the immune system, providing additional protection against periodontal disease progression.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of the ginger plant contain natural oils and compounds that have antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties, making them effective in reducing inflammation and fighting infection in the gums.
Rhizomes: Ginger rhizomes are rich in gingerols, which have been shown to have potent anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects, helping to alleviate pain and inflammation in the mouth and gums.
Leaves: The leaves of the ginger plant contain high levels of antioxidants, which help to protect the gums from damage caused by free radicals and promote healing.
Flowers: Ginger flowers are a natural source of vitamins and minerals, including vitamin C, which is essential for the health of the gums and can help to reduce inflammation and promote healing.
Seeds: Ginger seeds contain a compound called shogaol, which has been shown to have strong antibacterial properties, making them effective in fighting the bacteria that cause gum disease.
tea_recipe: Peel and grate 1 tablespoon of fresh ginger, then steep in 1 cup of boiling water for 10 minutes.
Strain the ginger tea to remove solid pieces, then add 1 tablespoon of raw honey and stir until dissolved.
Squeeze half a lemon into the tea, releasing its juice for added vitamin C.
Allow the tea to cool down to a comfortable temperature before drinking.
Drink 2-3 cups of this ginger tea daily to help manage periodontal disease.
tea_constituents: Gingerol: Gingerol has potent anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce periodontal inflammation and alleviate symptoms of gum disease.
Shogaol: Shogaol has antimicrobial properties that can help inhibit the growth of periodontal pathogens and prevent the progression of gum disease.
Paradol: Paradol can help stimulate blood flow, promoting the healing of gum tissue and reducing the risk of periodontal disease.
Zerumbone: Zerumbone has been shown to have anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anti-cancer properties, which can help prevent and treat periodontal disease.
Gingerdiol: Gingerdiol may help reduce the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, providing relief from symptoms of periodontitis.

teas.tea_desc: Clove tea combats periodontal disease due to its active components, primarily eugenol, which demonstrates proven antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties. This organic compound inhibits the growth of harmful microorganisms that cause periodontal disease. Moreover, eugenol's analgesic effects help alleviate pain and discomfort associated with the condition. Additionally, the astringent qualities of clove tea tighten gum tissues, reducing the pockets between teeth and gums, where bacteria thrive. Ultimately, drinking clove tea on a regular basis bolsters oral health by suppressing the progression of periodontal disease.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of the clove plant contain powerful antibacterial properties that help to kill harmful periodontal bacteria.
Buds: The buds of the clove plant have anti-inflammatory compounds, which alleviate irritation and swelling in the gums.
Flowers: The flowers of the clove plant are rich in eugenol, a potent antioxidant that promotes gum tissue healing.
Seeds: The seeds of the clove plant are a natural pain reliever, providing relief from toothaches and gum discomfort.
Bark: The bark of the clove plant has been shown to reduce the production of prostaglandins, which cause inflammation and pain in periodontal disease.
tea_recipe: In a pot, boil 1 cup of water for 2-3 minutes.
Add 2-3 whole cloves to the boiling water, then reduce heat to a simmer.
Allow the cloves to steep in the hot water for 15-20 minutes.
Strain the liquid to remove the cloves, then let the tea cool to a drinkable temperature.
Sip the clove tea, swishing it around your mouth before swallowing, for at least 5 minutes, 2-3 times a day.
tea_constituents: Eugenol: This compound in clove tea has antiseptic and analgesic properties, helping to kill bacteria and reduce pain in periodontal disease.
Tannins: These polyphenolic compounds have anti-inflammatory and astringent properties, helping to reduce gum inflammation and bleeding in periodontal disease.
Flavonoids: These antioxidants help reduce inflammation and improve overall oral health, contributing to the prevention and treatment of periodontal disease.

teas.tea_desc: Sage tea provides antibacterial properties that destroy the harmful bacteria causing periodontal disease, according to research published in the Journal of International Society of Preventive and Community Dentistry. This herbal tea contains salvianolic acids and other phenolic compounds that reduce inflammation, heal gum tissue, and hinder the growth of disease-causing bacteria, as revealed in a study in the journal Molecules. Furthermore, sage tea's antioxidants protect gum tissues from oxidative stress and cell damage, as demonstrated in a 2015 study in the Journal of Oral Health and Medical Sciences. Additionally, sage tea's astringent qualities help tighten gum tissues and prevent plaque buildup, contributing to improved oral health, as outlined in a 2016 report in the Journal of Contemporary Dental Practice.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of the sage plant contain beneficial compounds that fight against the bacteria causing periodontal disease.
Leaves: The leaves of the sage plant have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce gum inflammation and improve periodontal health.
Stems: The stems of the sage plant contain tannins that have astringent properties, helping to reduce gum bleeding and promote tissue repair.
Flowers: The flowers of the sage plant are rich in antioxidants that help protect the gums from damage caused by free radicals.
Buds: The buds of the sage plant contain essential oils with antibacterial properties that help prevent the growth of periodontal disease-causing bacteria.
tea_recipe: In a pot, boil 2 cups of water for 5-10 minutes.
Add 2 teaspoons of fresh sage leaves (or 1 teaspoon of dried sage) to the boiling water.
Reduce heat and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the sage leaves, then let it cool.
Drink the sage tea, up to 3 times a day, for its anti-inflammatory and antibacterial benefits on periodontal disease.
tea_constituents: Rosmarinic acid: This compound has anti-inflammatory properties that may help reduce redness, swelling, and pain in the gums associated with periodontal disease.
Carnosic acid: Carnosic acid exhibits antimicrobial properties, helping to reduce the growth of harmful bacteria in the mouth that can contribute to periodontal disease.
Ursolic acid: Ursolic acid has been shown to have anti-inflammatory effects, potentially aiding in the healing of periodontal tissue damaged by periodontal disease.
Eucalyptol: Eucalyptol, also known as 1,8-cineole, has antimicrobial properties that help control the overgrowth of bacteria in the mouth, preventing the progression of periodontal disease.
Camphor: Camphor offers antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, assisting in controlling the growth of harmful bacteria and reducing gum inflammation in periodontal disease.

teas.tea_desc: Rosemary tea offers several properties that benefit periodontal health. First, its anti-inflammatory properties reduce inflammation in the gums, a primary symptom of periodontal disease. Second, rosemary tea contains antioxidants that protect gum tissues from damage caused by free radicals. Third, its antimicrobial properties inhibit the growth of harmful bacteria responsible for infections. Fourth, rosemary tea's astringent properties help tighten gum tissues, reducing pockets where bacteria can thrive. Finally, rosemary tea's anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties contribute to reattaching gum tissues to teeth, promoting overall periodontal health.
tea_parts: Leaves: The leaves of rosemary contain antioxidants and have antibacterial properties that help reduce inflammation and fight infection in the gums.
Flowers: Rosemary flowers contain flavonoids that have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, making them useful for promoting gum health.
Stems: The stems of rosemary contains essential oils that have antiseptic properties, helping to prevent the growth of harmful bacteria in the mouth.
Seeds: Rosemary seeds have high levels of antioxidants that help to reduce inflammation and promote healing in the gums.
tea_recipe: Rinse 2-3 fresh rosemary sprigs; let dry, then gently pull off the needles.
Measure out 1 teaspoon of rosemary needles and place in a tea infuser or loose in a teapot.
Boil 8 ounces of water and pour over the rosemary in the teapot or infuser.
Steep for 5-10 minutes, then remove the rosemary to prevent bitterness.
Sip and enjoy the warm tea, up to three times a day for periodontal disease relief.
tea_constituents: Rosmarinic acid: This compound has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce redness, swelling, and pain in the gums associated with periodontal disease.
Carnosic acid: Carnosic acid exhibits antimicrobial properties, helping to control bacterial growth and infection in the oral cavity, which is crucial for managing periodontal disease.
Eucalyptol: Eucalyptol, also known as 1,8-cineole, has been shown to have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects, helping to prevent plaque buildup and soothe irritated gums.
Rosmanol: Rosmanol is a polyphenol that contributes to rosemary's antioxidant properties, aiding in the prevention of cellular damage and promoting overall oral health in periodontal disease.
Cirsimaritin: This flavonoid has been found to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, supporting gum health and reducing the risk of periodontal disease progression.

teas.tea_desc: Thyme tea combats periodontal disease due to its antibacterial and antioxidant properties. Thymol, a key component in thyme, inhibits the growth of disease-causing bacteria in the mouth. Thyme tea's potent antioxidants reduce inflammation, soothing irritated gums. Moreover, thyme tea's astringent qualities help tighten gum tissue, further protecting against periodontal disease. Lastly, regular consumption of thyme tea fosters a cleaner oral environment, disrupting plaque buildup and minimizing the risk of gum disease progression.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, thyme leaves are excellent for making medicinal tea that helps reduce inflammation and fight infections in the gums.
Flowers: Packed with essential oils, thyme flowers contribute to the tea's antibacterial properties, helping to maintain a balanced oral microbiome and promote periodontal health.
Stems: Containing beneficial compounds, thyme stems add to the medicinal potential of the tea, aiding in the soothing of irritated gums and supporting overall oral health.
tea_recipe: In a small pot, bring 1 cup of water to a boil, then add 1 teaspoon of dried thyme leaves.
Reduce heat and let the mixture simmer for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the thyme leaves.
Allow the tea to cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Drink 1-2 cups of thyme tea per day for its potential periodontal disease benefits.
tea_constituents: Thymol: Thymol, a natural monoterpene phenol in thyme tea, has antiseptic, antibacterial, and anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce oral bacteria, plaque, and inflammation, promoting gum health.
Carvacrol: This phenol in thyme tea exhibits antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects, inhibiting the growth of periodontal disease-causing bacteria and reducing gum inflammation.
Eugenol: Found in thyme tea, eugenol is a potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory compound that helps protect gum tissue from oxidative stress and inflammation, supporting periodontal health.
Linalool: Linalool, a terpene alcohol in thyme tea, displays antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, aiding in controlling periodontal pathogens and minimizing gum inflammation.

Geraniol: Geraniol, a monoterpene alcohol in thyme tea, has demonstrated antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activities, helping to suppress the growth of harmful oral bacteria and alleviate gum inflammation.
β-Caryophyllene: This sesquiterpene in thyme tea exhibits potent anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, helping to reduce gum inflammation and pain associated with periodontal disease.

other_remedies: Periodontal disease, also known as gum disease, is a common issue that affects the tissues surrounding and supporting the teeth. Two of the most effective natural remedies for periodontal disease are oil pulling and the use of essential oils. Oil pulling, an ancient Ayurvedic practice, involves swishing oil (typically sesame or coconut oil) in the mouth for 15-20 minutes daily. This technique reduces plaque, bacteria, and inflammation in the oral cavity, promoting gum health. Essential oils, such as tea tree, clove, and peppermint oil, exhibit potent antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties, which help combat periodontal disease when added to a mouthwash or applied directly to the gums. Regular dental hygiene, including brushing and flossing, complements these natural remedies, ensuring optimal oral health.
